title: The Rising Toll of Perfectionism
subtitle: 追求完美正在毁掉你：完美主义的隐形代价

paragraphs.en: When psychologist Jessica Pryor lived near an internationally renowned university, she once saw a student walking into a library holding a sleeping bag and a coffee maker. She has heard of graduate students spending 12 to 18 hours at a time in the lab. Their schedules are meant to be literally punishing: if they are scientists-in-training, they won't allow themselves to watch Netflix until their experiments start generating results.
paragraphs.zh: 心理学家杰西卡·普赖尔住在一所国际知名大学附近时，曾看到一名学生拿着睡袋和咖啡机走进图书馆。她听说有研究生一次在实验室待12到18小时。他们的日程安排简直是自我惩罚：如果是受训中的科学家，实验开始出结果之前他们不会允许自己看Netflix。

words.meaning: adv. 确实地，毫不夸张地

paragraphs.en: Relationships become estranged — people stop inviting them to social gatherings, which leads them to spend even more time in the lab, Pryor told me. Along with other therapists, Pryor is trying to sound the alarm about a tendency among young adults to strive for perfection in their work — sometimes at any cost. Though often portrayed as a positive trait, extreme perfectionism can lead to depression, anxiety, and even suicide. What's more, perfectionism seems to be on the rise.
paragraphs.zh: 普赖尔告诉我：“人际关系变得疏远——人们不再邀请他们参加社交聚会，这导致他们在实验室里花更多时间。”普赖尔与其他治疗师一道，正试图对年轻人工作中追求完美——有时不惜一切代价——的倾向敲响警钟。尽管常被描绘为积极品质，极端完美主义却可导致抑郁、焦虑甚至自杀。更重要的是，完美主义似乎正在抬头。

paragraphs.en: In a study of thousands of college students published earlier this year, Thomas Curran and Andrew Hill found that today's students report higher levels of perfectionism than students did during the 1990s or early 2000s. They measured three types: self-oriented, or a desire to be perfect; socially prescribed, or a desire to live up to others' expectations; and other-oriented, or holding others to unrealistic standards. From 1989 to 2016, self-oriented scores increased by 10 percent, socially prescribed by 33 percent, and other-oriented by 16 percent.
paragraphs.zh: 在今年早些时候发表的一项涵盖数千名大学生的研究中，托马斯·柯兰和安德鲁·希尔发现，如今的大学生报告的完美主义水平高于20世纪90年代或21世纪初的大学生。他们测量了三种类型：自我导向型，即渴望自身完美；社会期许型，即渴望满足他人期望；他人导向型，即以不切实际的标准要求他人。从1989年到2016年，自我导向得分上升10%，社会期许得分飙升33%，他人导向得分增加16%。

paragraphs.en: A person living with an other-oriented perfectionist might feel criticized for not doing household chores exactly the right way. One of the most common things couples argue about is the proper way of loading the dishwasher, says Amy Bach, a psychologist. Curran describes socially prescribed perfectionism as 'My self-esteem is contingent on what other people think.' His study didn't examine causal reasons, but he assumes the rise of standardized testing and social media might play a role.
paragraphs.zh: 与一个他人导向的完美主义者同住，可能会因没有以完全“正确”的方式做家务而受到批评。心理学家艾米·巴赫说，夫妻间最常争论的问题之一就是如何正确摆放洗碗机。柯兰将社会期许型完美主义描述为“我的自尊取决于他人的看法”。他的研究未探究因果关系，但他推测标准化考试和社交媒体的兴起可能起了作用。

words.meaning: adj. 取决于……的，视……而定的

paragraphs.en: These days, LinkedIn alerts us when our rival gets a new job, and Instagram lets us know how well liked our lives are compared with a friend's. In an opinion piece, Curran and Hill argue that society has become more dog-eat-dog. Over the last 50 years, public interest and civic responsibility have been eroded, replaced by a focus on self-interest and competition. We strive for perfection because we feel we must in order to get ahead.
paragraphs.zh: 如今，当竞争对手找到新工作时领英会提醒我们，Instagram则让我们知道自己的生活与朋友相比有多“受欢迎”。在一篇评论文章中，柯兰和希尔认为社会已变得更加弱肉强食。过去50年里，公共利益和公民责任逐渐被侵蚀，取而代之的是对自身利益和竞争的关注。我们追求完美，因为我们觉得必须如此才能出人头地。

paragraphs.en: Michael Brustein, a clinical psychologist in Manhattan, says when he first began practicing he was surprised by how prevalent perfectionism was among his clients. He sees it in entrepreneurs, artists, and tech employees. 'You're in New York because you're ambitious, you have this need to strive,' he says. 'But then your whole identity gets wrapped into a goal.' Perfectionism can, of course, be a positive force. Think of professional athletes who train aggressively for ever-higher levels.
paragraphs.zh: 曼哈顿临床心理学家迈克尔·布鲁斯坦说，刚开始执业时他对客户中完美主义的普遍程度感到惊讶。他在企业家、艺术家和技术员工身上都看到这一点。他说：“你来纽约是因为有抱负，有奋斗的需求。但随后你的整个身份就被裹挟进一个目标里。”当然，完美主义可以是一种积极力量。想想那些为更高水平而刻苦训练的职业运动员。

paragraphs.en: In well-adjusted perfectionism, someone who doesn't get the gold can forget the setback and move on. In maladaptive perfectionism, people make an archive of all their failures. They revisit these archives constantly, thinking, 'I need to make myself feel terrible so I don't do this again.' Then they double down, raising the expectation bar even higher, which increases the likelihood of defeat. Next comes failure, shame, and pushing yourself even harder toward more impossible goals.
paragraphs.zh: 在适应性良好的完美主义中，未夺金的人能忘记挫折继续前行。而在适应不良的完美主义中，人们会归档所有失败。他们不断重温这些档案，心想“我得让自己难受，这样就不会重蹈覆辙”。然后他们加倍努力，将期望标准提得更高，反而增加了失败的可能。接下来是失败、羞耻，以及朝着更不可能的目标更加拼命。

paragraphs.en: Meeting them becomes an all or nothing premise. Pryor offered this example: 'Even if I'm an incredible attorney, if I don't make partner in the same pacing as one of my colleagues, clearly that means I'm a failure.' Brustein says his perfectionist clients tend to devalue their accomplishments, so each achievement's high lasts only a short time, like a gas tank with a hole in it. If the boss says you did a great job, it's because he doesn't know anything. If the audience likes your work, it's because they're too stupid to know what good art is.
paragraphs.zh: 实现目标变成了“全有或全无”的前提。普赖尔举例说：“即便我是出色的律师，如果我没有以和同事相同的节奏成为合伙人，那显然意味着我是个失败者。”布鲁斯坦说他的完美主义客户倾向于贬低自己的成就，因此每次达成目标后的兴奋感只持续片刻，就像“油箱破了个洞”。老板夸你做得好，那是因为他什么都不懂。观众喜欢你的作品，那是因为他们太蠢，不知道什么是真正的好艺术。

paragraphs.en: But therapists say there are different ways perfectionism manifests. Some perfectionists always push themselves forward. Others actually fall behind on work, unable to complete assignments unless they are perfect. Or they might handicap their performance ahead of time, partying until 2 a.m. before the final so there's a ready excuse for a low grade. While educators have convinced students of the need to be high performing, they haven't prepared them for the inevitability of failure.
paragraphs.zh: 但治疗师说完美主义有不同的表现方式。有些完美主义者总是鞭策自己前进。另一些则实际上落后于工作，除非做到完美否则无法完成任务。或者他们可能提前给自己的表现设障，期末考试前夜狂欢到凌晨两点，为低分找好现成借口。教育者虽已让学生相信需要表现出色，却没有让他们为不可避免的失败做好准备。

paragraphs.en: Instead of praises like 'You're so smart,' parents and educators should say 'You really stuck with it,' Pryor says, to emphasize perseverance over intrinsic talent. Pryor notes that many clients worry she'll turn them into degenerate couch potatoes. Instead, she helps them think through which parts of perfectionism to keep and which to lose. Bach tells her students, 'Aim high, but get comfortable with good enough.' When they miss an award, she encourages them to remember that one outcome is not a basis for a broad conclusion about their intelligence or potential.
paragraphs.zh: 普赖尔说，家长和教育者不应夸“你真聪明”，而应说“你真的坚持下来了”，以强调毅力胜过天赋。普赖尔指出许多客户担心她会把他们变成堕落的沙发土豆。相反，她帮助他们思考哪些完美主义部分值得保留，哪些正在毁掉生活应该舍弃。巴赫告诉学生：“目标高远，但要安于足够好。”当他们错失奖项时，她鼓励他们记住，一次结果不足以对智力或潜能下定论。

paragraphs.en: The treatment for perfectionism might be as simple as having patients keep logs of things they can be proud of, or having them behave imperfectly in small ways, just to see how it feels. 'We might have them hang the towels crooked or wear some clothing inside out,' says Martin Antony. Brustein likes to get his clients to create values important to them, then shift their focus to living according to those values rather than achieving specific goals. It's about going to yoga class because you like to be the kind of person who takes care of herself, not about doing a headstand.
paragraphs.zh: 治疗完美主义的方法可能简单到让患者记录自己引以为豪的事，或让他们在小事上表现得不够完美，看看感觉如何。马丁·安东尼说：“我们可能会让他们把毛巾挂歪，或者把衣服反着穿。”布鲁斯坦喜欢让客户确立对他们重要的价值观，然后将注意力转向按照这些价值观生活，而非实现具体目标。这关乎去上瑜伽课是因为喜欢成为照顾自己的人，而不是为了做头倒立。

paragraphs.en: But he warns that some people go into therapy expecting too much — an instant transformation from a pathological perfectionist to a still high-achieving non-perfectionist. They try to be perfect, in other words, at no longer being perfect.
paragraphs.zh: 但他警告，有些人接受治疗时期望过高——期待自己瞬间从病态完美主义者转变为仍然高成就的非完美主义者。换句话说，他们试图在“不再完美”这件事上也做到完美。
